summary: The post revisits Yishan Wong's influential advice about promoting managers from within and argues that companies often fail because they overlook the critical requirement of mentoring new managers. Fournier observes that startups promote technical contributors to management roles but provide no guidance, expecting great managers to emerge naturally. She reflects on her own journey, admitting that despite confidence in her technical skills and leadership inclination, she only began understanding management's complexity after extensive coaching. The core argument is that promotion from within only works if paired with intensive mentorship and accountability for new managers' development.
key_points: Companies adopt 'promote from within' but skip the essential mentoring component that makes it successful
Great managers are made through patient teaching, observation of successes and failures, and being held accountable for their mistakes
Startup culture's reverence for founders and early employees often prevents the honest feedback new managers need
The author's own experience shows that even strong technical skills and natural leadership inclination are insufficient without coaching
Without proper support, promoting from within offers no advantage over hiring experienced external managers
money_quotes: Great managers are truly not born. They are made, and usually made through both being sat down and patiently taught the ways to effectively lead projects and people, but also through observing both successes and failures.
The cult of personality around founders and early employees can work against the one thing necessary to make 'promote from within' successful: some form of external help.
I came into this job two years ago thinking that given my natural willingness to be in charge and my strong technical skills I would be a great manager. Haha!
Truthfully I'm only now getting to the point where I have an inkling of all the things I don't know, and a large part of that is thanks to a ton of coaching.
We've taken these ideas that Yishan so eloquently voiced, that culture is paramount, and elevated them to high status, while forgetting that there is a lot to learn to be a successful manager.
Don't cheap out on the process by forgetting that these new managers and leaders need help, need training, need to be held responsible for both the good and the bad.
key_insight: Promoting managers from within only succeeds when paired with intensive mentorship and accountability—without external coaching and honest feedback, internal promotions fail just as often as external hires risk cultural misalignment.
